Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just drift through the day doing other things. When I did study, I preferred listening to lecture recordings while doing other stuff around my place. I'd sometimes have these bursts where I'd cram a lot in, but mostly I just let things happen organically without much structure.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and honestly, how I was feeling that day. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then right before something was due, I'd suddenly become super productive. I'm not great with consistency—I get bored easily and struggle to maintain regular habits. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced any kind of pattern.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about an upcoming deadline or just pure boredom. Sometimes I'd log in just to scroll through and see what was happening, not really intending to do anything productive. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more because there was something slightly entertaining about them. But mostly, I needed some external push to actually open the LMS.
Q4: The gamified elements actually helped make it more routine for a while. I started checking in to see my progress on those tasks, which felt less boring than regular coursework. But once the novelty wore off, I'd go back to my old habits of sporadic logins. It never really became a solid routine—more like occasional bursts of engagement when something caught my attention.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them while doing other things, which made studying feel less tedious. The discussion posts were also useful because hearing other students' perspectives sometimes clicked things into place for me. I liked anything I could passively absorb rather than actively work through.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. Reading just doesn't hold my attention—my mind wanders constantly. I'd also skip a lot of the optional practice activities because they felt like busywork. If something wasn't graded or didn't seem immediately relevant to exams, it went to the bottom of my priority list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was either directly tied to my grade or actually interesting enough to hold my attention. The gamified tasks worked because they added some element of challenge or reward that made them less monotonous. I also valued anything that was efficient—if I could learn something quickly without dragging through pages of text, I was more likely to engage.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones had this weight of consequences attached, so I'd eventually get to them. Optional stuff just sat there, and I'd think 'I should do that' but never actually would. There's something about knowing something doesn't count that makes my brain immediately deprioritize it.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I was out or doing chores. I also used some YouTube videos to supplement topics I found confusing. Sometimes I'd talk through concepts with friends from my extracurriculars who were in similar courses. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours thinking through problems or mentally reviewing lecture content while doing other things—like walking or working out. I'd also sometimes have long conversations with classmates about course material that happened over text or in person. None of that shows up anywhere, but it was definitely part of how I processed information.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd download the audio lectures once and then listen to them multiple times offline. Same with some of the slides—I'd screenshot them and review them on my phone later. The LMS would just show one access, but I was actually going back to that material repeatedly. I preferred having things accessible without needing to log in every time.
Q12: There were definitely weeks where my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't bring myself to engage with anything academic. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because even looking at it stressed me out. Sometimes competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars pulled my attention away. The boredom also made it hard—when content felt dry, I'd just mentally check out.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. With limited resources, I'm often juggling things that drain my energy. When I was tired, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion also made me disengage—if I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes just close the tab rather than push through. It felt easier to avoid than to struggle.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were plenty of times I'd have videos playing but I was completely zoned out, thinking about other things. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. My body was present but my mind was somewhere else entirely. The LMS would count that as engagement, but I wasn't actually learning anything in those moments.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially assignments that felt tedious or overwhelming. Even knowing they were important, I'd tell myself I'd do them later and then panic when later arrived. My anxiety made it worse—sometimes the thought of starting something felt so daunting that avoidance seemed like the easier option. I'd fill my time with other things to justify not doing coursework.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me actually complete work. I'd have this pattern of ignoring things until the deadline loomed, then cramming everything in at the last minute. It was stressful but somehow I'd pull through. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything—they were the external pressure I needed to overcome my natural tendency to drift.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always in productive ways. Good feedback boosted my mood and made me more likely to engage afterward. But critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid the course for a while. I'd eventually come back, but there was definitely a delay while I processed my feelings about it.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and frustrating. Having clear modules helped me know what to focus on, but the sheer number of online courses I was taking made everything blur together. The gamified elements actually helped break up the monotony and gave me something to latch onto. But overall, I felt like I was just going through motions rather than deeply engaging with the structure.
Q19: The audio and video content made engagement easier since that matches how I learn best. The gamified tasks also helped because they added some variety. But the heavy reliance on reading materials made things harder for me—I'd just skip those entirely. Also, having so many courses at once made it hard to give any single one my full attention.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—browsing around, watching videos passively, maybe even participating in discussions, but then struggling to actually complete the assignments. It might be anxiety about submitting work, or feeling like the assignments are too tedious compared to just exploring content. High activity doesn't mean someone is actually producing anything.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I performed well on exams despite not being super active in the LMS because I was learning in other ways—listening to downloaded lectures, talking with peers, thinking through concepts on my own. The LMS only captures a fraction of actual learning. Someone can be studying effectively without it showing up in the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in and what they click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is actually absorbing information or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline learning, the conversations, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what I'm learning and feeling connected to the material somehow. It's not just about completing tasks—it's about being mentally present and curious. For me, true engagement happens when something captures my interest enough that I want to understand it, not just get through it. That's rare, honestly, but it's what real engagement feels like.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through listening and conversations rather than traditional studying. It wouldn't capture my anxiety and how that affected my engagement patterns. It also wouldn't show that despite appearing disengaged at times, I was actually processing and learning in my own way. My internal experience of the course was much richer than my digital footprint suggests.
